  <meta name="description" content="In Santiago, a quiet mental health crisis has been unfolding—one where too many people have faced long waits or no access to care at all.

I had the privilege of being part of the team helping to lead an effort to change that.

Healthy Santiago is an initiative that brings together cross-sector leaders to rethink how mental health care is delivered at the city level—particularly for young people and older adults. Working alongside partners including Asociación Chilena De Seguridad, CETA Global, and local governments in Municipalidad de Renca and Municipalidad de Providencia, we focused on a model with the potential to meet people where they are: mental health task sharing.

At its core, task sharing is a simple but powerful idea—training trusted providers, from school counselors to social workers, to deliver evidence-based mental health interventions. In practice, it represents a fundamental redesign of access.

And it’s working.

The program has already reached thousands of individuals and their families, closing up to 45% of the access gap for the populations served. But what’s equally important is what this signals at a system level: that access at scale is possible when we rethink who delivers care.

There is also a clear investment case. Our analysis shows that task sharing can generate a social return of 7–17x—reducing healthcare costs, supporting workforce participation, and improving education and life outcomes. This is what it looks like when mental health is treated as an investment, not a cost.

We have several new publications that build on this experience:

➡️ “The future is shared: Financing task-sharing programs in mental health”
Playbook outlining financing models and system conditions to scale and sustain task sharing
https://lnkd.in/e2-NmPeW

➡️ The Healthy Santiago impact story
Closer look at how task sharing is delivering impact on the ground
https://lnkd.in/eCrtYiKN

➡️ Mental Health and AI Field Guide
Approaches for leveraging AI to scale task sharing, published last summer with Google and Grand Challenges Canada
https://lnkd.in/eF_ZHs6a

The opportunity is substantial: an estimated 660 million people with mild to moderate conditions could benefit from task sharing. If scaled, this approach could help regain 18.6 million years of healthy life and unlock ~$350 billion in economic value globally.

The mental health access challenges faced in Santiago are all too familiar of a story in communities around the world. What we helped build in Santiago reinforces a broader point: task sharing is not a temporary solution to workforce shortages—it is a pathway to more resilient, inclusive health systems, and to stronger communities overall. And the business case is there for cross-sector leaders to lean in.

#InvestInMentalHealth #HealthSystems #GlobalHealth #HealthInCities

Kana Enomoto Javier Valenzuela Clara Gianola Katerina Mamay Lewis Laura Murray Rodrigo de la Calle Juan Luis Moreno Claudio Castro Salas Jaime Bellolio">

  <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        attributed-text-segment-list__content-truncated" dir="ltr" data-test-id="main-feed-activity-card__commentary">In Santiago, a quiet mental health crisis has been unfolding—one where too many people have faced long waits or no access to care at all.

I had the privilege of being part of the team helping to lead an effort to change that.

Healthy Santiago is an initiative that brings together cross-sector leaders to rethink how mental health care is delivered at the city level—particularly for young people and older adults. Working alongside partners including <a class="link" href="https://cl.linkedin.com/company/asociaci%C3%B3n-chilena-de-seguridad?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">Asociación Chilena De Seguridad</a>, <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/company/ceta-global?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">CETA Global</a>, and local governments in <a class="link" href="https://cl.linkedin.com/company/municipalidad-de-renca?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">Municipalidad de Renca</a> and <a class="link" href="https://cl.linkedin.com/company/ilustre-municipalidad-de-providencia?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">Municipalidad de Providencia</a>, we focused on a model with the potential to meet people where they are: mental health task sharing.

At its core, task sharing is a simple but powerful idea—training trusted providers, from school counselors to social workers, to deliver evidence-based mental health interventions. In practice, it represents a fundamental redesign of access.

And it’s working.

The program has already reached thousands of individuals and their families, closing up to 45% of the access gap for the populations served. But what’s equally important is what this signals at a system level: that access at scale is possible when we rethink who delivers care.

There is also a clear investment case. Our analysis shows that task sharing can generate a social return of 7–17x—reducing healthcare costs, supporting workforce participation, and improving education and life outcomes. This is what it looks like when mental health is treated as an investment, not a cost.

We have several new publications that build on this experience:

➡️ “The future is shared: Financing task-sharing programs in mental health”
Playbook outlining financing models and system conditions to scale and sustain task sharing
<a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https%3A%2F%2Flnkd%2Ein%2Fe2-NmPeW&urlhash=68R5&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" rel="nofollow" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">https://lnkd.in/e2-NmPeW</a>

➡️ The Healthy Santiago impact story
Closer look at how task sharing is delivering impact on the ground
<a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https%3A%2F%2Flnkd%2Ein%2FeCrtYiKN&urlhash=e71w&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" rel="nofollow" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">https://lnkd.in/eCrtYiKN</a>

➡️ Mental Health and AI Field Guide
Approaches for leveraging AI to scale task sharing, published last summer with Google and Grand Challenges Canada
<a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https%3A%2F%2Flnkd%2Ein%2FeF_ZHs6a&urlhash=gx4c&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" rel="nofollow" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">https://lnkd.in/eF_ZHs6a</a>

The opportunity is substantial: an estimated 660 million people with mild to moderate conditions could benefit from task sharing. If scaled, this approach could help regain 18.6 million years of healthy life and unlock ~$350 billion in economic value globally.

The mental health access challenges faced in Santiago are all too familiar of a story in communities around the world. What we helped build in Santiago reinforces a broader point: task sharing is not a temporary solution to workforce shortages—it is a pathway to more resilient, inclusive health systems, and to stronger communities overall. And the business case is there for cross-sector leaders to lean in.

  <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
         comment__text babybear:mt-0.5" dir="ltr">Thank you for sharing this incredibly important work <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/in/ericahutchins?trk=public_post_comment-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post_comment-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">Erica Hutchins Coe</a>
A powerful example of what can be achieved when communities, health systems, and cross-sector partners come together to rethink access to mental health care. 
The impact already being achieved through task sharing demonstrates not only innovation, but genuine leadership in creating more inclusive and sustainable support systems for people who need them most.
The evidence around improved access, stronger community outcomes, and long-term social and economic value is compelling. 
Initiatives like this reinforce that mental health investment is both a social responsibility and a strategic priority.
Hopefully, the success and learnings from this initiative can be shared across the region, with neighbouring countries collaborating and cross-sharing experiences, insights, and strategies that have proven effective. There is enormous value in building collective capability and learning together to strengthen mental health systems globally.
Congratulations to everyone involved in driving such meaningful change.</p>

  <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
         comment__text babybear:mt-0.5" dir="ltr">What stands out in this work is the shift from treating mental health as a downstream consequence to recognizing cognitive and emotional capacity as foundational infrastructure for long-term societal resilience and human development.

The earlier systems are able to protect and strengthen that capacity, the more we preserve the conditions that allow people to learn, adapt, participate, and contribute over time — something that is becoming increasingly central to how institutions think about long-term brain capital and continuity.

Important leadership from <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/in/ericahutchins?trk=public_post_comment-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post_comment-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">Erica Hutchins Coe</a>, Kana Enomoto, and the broader team helping move this conversation from awareness into real institutional action.</p>

  <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        " dir="ltr" data-test-id="main-feed-activity-card__commentary">Reading through the 2026–27 Federal Budget and broader mental health funding announcements, one thing stood out to me very clearly. By the time many Australians enter formal mental health support systems, they are already significantly unwell.

They’ve often moved through, prolonged stress, reduced capacity, burnout,
relationship strain, workplace pressure, financial distress, isolation and before finally reaching crisis supports, hospitals, income support systems, or leaving the workforce altogether.

My perspective, that raises a bigger question.

➡️ How early are we actually intervening?

Because most organisations genuinely want to support mental health.
➡️ They have wellbeing initiatives.
➡️ They offer EAP.
➡️ They run awareness campaigns.
➡️ They encourage conversations.

But too often, intervention still happens after someone is already struggling.
➡️ After behaviours change.
➡️ After performance drops.
➡️ After relationships deteriorate.
➡️ After someone disengages from work or life.

At that point, we’re no longer preventing harm. We’re responding to it.

What stood out in this Budget review was the continued gap around psychosocial supports outside the NDIS, despite growing demand and increasing complexity across the community sector.  Because clinical care alone is not enough, psychosocial supports are often what help people:

➡️ to stay connected.
➡️ to remain employed.
➡️ maintain stable housing.
➡️ to recover earlier.
➡️ and avoid entering deeper crisis systems altogether.

Without those supports, the pressure doesn’t disappear it re-directs to:
➡️ emergency departments
➡️ families and carers
➡️  police and first responders
➡️ homelessness services
➡️ workplaces
➡️ stretched community organisations

In my view, mental health reform can no longer be treated as only a healthcare conversation. It’s also:

🤔  a workplace issue.
🤔 a psychosocial safety issue.
🤔 a leadership issue.
🤔 a community resilience issue.
🤔  an economic issue.

Particularly as we continue seeing rising psychological injury claims, workforce fatigue, burnout and increasing complexity across frontline sectors.
The opportunity sits much earlier than crisis response in how work is designed how we has leaders manage demand and expectations, importantly how people feel psychologically safe to speak up early and in whether psychosocial risks are identified and managed before harm escalates.

Because once someone exits the workforce due to psychological injury, the pathway back becomes significantly harder. If we’re serious about prevention, we need to move beyond reactive systems and invest earlier in psychosocial supports, our prevention and early intervention, determining psychologically safe workplaces, having sustainable community mental health services and stronger support pathways outside crisis systems.

    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        " dir="ltr" data-test-id="main-feed-activity-card__commentary">The 'Positive Mental Health & Wellbeing' subgroup of the Tallaght HANA Collaborative is co-chaired by <a class="link" href="https://ie.linkedin.com/in/triona-mcnicholas-96152086?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">Triona McNicholas</a> from <a class="link" href="https://ie.linkedin.com/company/health-service-executive?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">Health Service Executive</a> and <a class="link" href="https://ie.linkedin.com/in/kim-dempsey-456bb338?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">Kim Dempsey</a> <a class="link" href="https://ie.linkedin.com/company/south-dublin-county-partnership?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">South Dublin County Partnership</a>. Today they hosted a networking event within Tallaght to facilitate services to come together, to learn about each other and share ideas on how they may work together more in the future to support the people of Tallaght. This network meeting was ably facilitated by <a class="link" href="https://ie.linkedin.com/in/michael-foley-25bb781b?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">Michael Foley</a> from <a class="link" href="https://ie.linkedin.com/school/trinity-college-dublin/?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">Trinity College Dublin</a> Civic Engagement for Societal Impact. We heard from four services within Tallaght: 

1. 'Heads Up' Karen Keyes spoke about their evidence based mental health CBT informed service available within the community. 
2. <a class="link" href="https://ie.linkedin.com/in/tara-deacy-91086237?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">Tara Deacy</a> from <a class="link" href="https://ie.linkedin.com/company/south-dublin-county-partnership?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">South Dublin County Partnership</a> spoke about social prescribing within the Slaintecare Healthy Communities, and how its about 'whats important to people' and not 'whats wrong with them' is a guiding principle of theirs to support people in the community to get/stay connected. 
3. <a class="link" href="https://ie.linkedin.com/company/for-ige?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">Foróige</a> 's <a class="link" href="https://ie.linkedin.com/in/jason-canning-psychology?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">Jason Canning</a> and Noeleen O'Brien highlighted their drop in services which a young person can available of (with no referral) plus referral options for healthcare practitioners. They also have a really cool 'podcast club' which has been a massive hit. 
4. <a class="link" href="https://ie.linkedin.com/company/alone?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">ALONE</a>'s Natasha Kinsella spoke of the increase in social isolation and loneliness that is being experienced within communities and what is on offer within Tallaght. For example, volunteers will visit and call people to break up their isolation, opportunities existing for people to get support for adovcating for housing, pragmatic guidance like a list of local reliable tradesmen etc. <a class="link" href="https://ie.linkedin.com/company/alone?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">ALONE</a> are offering a holistic approach in communities. 

What struck me most today was these services are working towards the same goal: creating a more connected, compassionate and supportive Tallaght.
Real community wellbeing happens when services know each other, trust each other, and work together around what matters to people. Today felt like an important reminder that Tallaght has both the expertise and the commitment to make that happen. <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Ftallaght&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">#Tallaght</a> <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fcommunitywellbeing&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">#CommunityWellbeing</a> <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fmentalhealth&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">#MentalHealth</a> <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fsocialprescribing&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">#SocialPrescribing</a> <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fcollaboration&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">#Collaboration</a> <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fhana&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">#HANA</a> 
To learn more about the research that underpins these communities efforts 'Health Assets and Needs Assessment 'HANA' in Tallaght Project' go to: <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https%3A%2F%2Flnkd%2Ein%2FeRxPbGKG&urlhash=7AIn&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" rel="nofollow" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">https://lnkd.in/eRxPbGKG</a>


<a class="link" href="https://ie.linkedin.com/in/professor-jo-hanna-ivers-b8634419?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">Professor Jo-Hanna Ivers</a> <a class="link" href="https://ie.linkedin.com/in/susan-smith-a7a0aa23b?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">Susan Smith</a> <a class="link" href="https://ie.linkedin.com/in/sara-burke-51b3908b?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">Sara Burke</a> <a class="link" href="https://ie.linkedin.com/in/orlagh-claffey-865b6293?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">Orlagh Claffey</a> <a class="link" href="https://ie.linkedin.com/in/niamh-gavin-9876b06?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">Niamh Gavin</a> <a class="link" href="https://ie.linkedin.com/company/tallaghtuniversityhospital?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">Tallaght University Hospital</a> <a class="link" href="https://ie.linkedin.com/company/south-dublin-county-council?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">South Dublin County Council</a> <a class="link" href="https://ie.linkedin.com/company/twcdi.ie?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">The Childhood Development Initiative</a></p>

  <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        " dir="ltr" data-test-id="main-feed-activity-card__commentary"><a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/company/el-futuro-inc.?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">El Futuro</a> provides culturally responsive mental health and substance use services for the Latino community with direct clinical services and virtual appointments; training, education, and capacity building; and community engagement work. Molly Hayes, MEd, LCMHCS, is the director of clinical enhancement services for El Futuro.

“Oftentimes in the Latino community there’s a sense that one is weak or not capable if there’s a struggle in mental health capacity or with substance use, like it’s a personal deficit or like someone has done something wrong,” Hayes says. “That can look like hiding symptoms, not sharing experiences, or not reaching out to family members, friends, or community members for support.”

El Futuro strives to overcome such cultural barriers by showing up in community and talking about mental health care and wellness. “Having those experiences be normalized and validated takes us a long way toward destigmatization,” Hayes says. “Hearing those stories in all of the spaces where folks are living their lives—be that in a faith community, in a community center, in our neighborhoods, at the grocery store, or at the fiestas that we go to—we try to have information about mental wellness and about needing support become just a normal part of the everyday experience.

“Culturally responsive mental health care is a community investment. It looks like a whole community coming together to destigmatize and to speak openly about mental wellness. So, that involves community events. It looks like gardening and green spaces. It looks like camps for kids. It looks like parent nights with parents coming together. That community presence is incredibly powerful, because that’s where people live their lives.”

Culturally respectful practices by mental health professionals are equally critical. “We need to understand how to incorporate identity, culture, and need into every clinical conversation, so that each person is heard, respected, and understood within their individual context,” Hayes says. “It’s not just ‘I speak Spanish, you speak Spanish.’ Really understanding all the contextual clues—that’s the care that we need.

"There are multitudes within this umbrella term of ‘Latino,’ even within North Carolina, ... (and) folks are traumatized. The level of anxiety in community has skyrocketed. There’s more isolation. People are scared to do the things that they need to do in their daily lives. But the resilience, strength, and solidarity of the Latino community persist.”

  <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        " dir="ltr" data-test-id="main-feed-activity-card__commentary">Government to Transform Mental Health Care with New Strategy 
 
Frontline workers, clinicians and mental health experts are being invited to share their views on how to transform mental health care for children and adults in England, as the government launches a call for evidence to shape its once-in-a-generation cross-government mental health strategy.

The strategy will drive a fundamental shift towards prevention - treating people earlier and faster, and supporting those with mental health conditions to live a full life and stay active in education, work, family life and their communities.

Demand for mental health services has risen rapidly, particularly among children and young people, who often face long waiting times for the care they need. Around 1 in 5 people are now affected by a common mental health condition.

Improving mental health care is a priority in the government’s manifesto and 10 Year Health Plan and the government has hit its target to hire 8,500 extra mental health workers 3 years ahead of schedule. This year, NHS mental health spending is forecast to reach a record £16.1 billion - a real-terms increase of around £140 million on last year.

On top of this, we are making £473 million available over the next 4 years for mental health emergency departments, community-based mental health centres and wider capital projects, accelerating the rollout of Mental Health Support Teams in schools and colleges and expanding community‑based support via Early Support and Young Futures Hubs.

But despite record investment and significant workforce growth, the current system remains reactive, fragmented and inconsistent. Support varies based on postcode, and too often people only get help when they reach crisis point. The mental health strategy will address this by setting new direction for the mental health system so that it responds earlier and more proportionately.

Baroness Merron, Minister for Mental Health, said: “This government believes that mental health should be treated with the same seriousness as physical health, yet too many people across the country are struggling to get the support they need, when they need it.

“Alongside record investment in mental health services and more mental health workers than ever in the NHS, this strategy will give mental health the attention it deserves and set us on a new direction - one that focuses on earlier help, faster access and a whole-system approach.

“We want to hear from everyone with a stake in getting this right, including frontline clinicians, service providers, and people with lived experience of mental health conditions, so that we can build a system that truly works for everyone.”

Mark Rowland, Chief Executive at the Mental Health Foundation, said: “This is a big moment for England’s mental health. For many years, we’ve been calling for a radical shift towards a cross-government approach with a focus on prevention, and we look forward to contributing to the plan’s success. 

“O…</p>

  <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        " dir="ltr" data-test-id="main-feed-activity-card__commentary">MEN DON'T NEED TO BE FIXED. THEY NEED TO BE MET WHERE THEY ARE.

Today, as we observe conversations around men's mental health, I am reminded that behind every statistic is a human being, a brother, a father, a friend, a colleague, or a son who may be silently struggling.

Research shows that millions of men live with depression, yet many never seek help. Men continue to die by suicide at significantly higher rates than women, and many experience mental distress through substance abuse, risk-taking behaviors, aggression, social withdrawal, and isolation rather than openly expressing emotional pain.

As Public Health professionals and students, we must recognize that mental health interventions cannot be one-size-fits-all. For too long, society has expected men to remain strong, silent, and self-reliant, often making vulnerability appear as weakness. The result is a culture where many men suffer in silence.

What stands out from the evidence is that men do not need to be "fixed." They need supportive environments that allow them to speak, connect, and seek help without fear of judgment.

Mental health promotion for men should focus on:

• Building supportive social networks and friendships.
• Encouraging help-seeking behavior through positive messaging.
• Integrating mental health services into workplaces, sports clubs, schools, and community settings.
• Challenging harmful stereotypes while promoting positive aspects of masculinity such as responsibility, resilience, purpose, and community support.
• Creating culturally inclusive interventions that reach all men, especially those from underserved communities.

One important lesson for Public Health is that social connectedness is not simply a social benefit—it is a protective health factor. Strong relationships reduce the risk of depression, substance abuse, and suicidal thoughts.

As future Public Health leaders, our responsibility is to design systems and communities that meet men where they are, rather than expecting them to fit into systems that were never designed with their experiences in mind.

The men in our lives may not always say they are struggling. Silence should never be mistaken for wellbeing.

Let us continue building communities where seeking support is seen as strength, not weakness.

  <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        " dir="ltr" data-test-id="main-feed-activity-card__commentary">Mental Health Awareness Week 2026 runs May 11 through May 17, and this year the message has shifted in a profound way. The official theme is not simply "talk about it." It is Action, and that distinction matters more than ever before.

For over two decades, mental health campaigns focused on breaking the silence surrounding emotional struggle. That work changed public discourse in meaningful ways. But in 2026, communities are confronting a harder truth: awareness revealed the full scale of the crisis. It did not build the systems needed to address it.
The data is difficult to ignore. According to the CDC, one in three U.S. high school students reports that their mental health was poor most or all of the time. Forty percent experience persistent sadness or hopelessness. One in five seriously considered suicide. These are not distant statistics. They represent students, coworkers, neighbors, and family members navigating profound emotional strain with far too little support available to them.

Here in Minnesota, the challenge is equally urgent. Despite ranking among the nation's healthiest states, Minnesota continues to face significant behavioral health workforce shortages, uneven access to culturally competent care, and growing pressure on crisis response systems. Rural communities and underserved populations carry a disproportionate burden. Educators are increasingly functioning as emotional first responders. Healthcare workers are burning out while trying to care for others under sustained pressure.

The Mental Health Foundation's 2026 campaign organizes action across three levels: personal, meaning protecting your own wellbeing through boundaries, rest, and self-care; interpersonal, meaning building psychologically safe environments in your workplace, family, and community; and structural, meaning advocating for expanded access to care, reduced wait times, improved insurance coverage, and stronger behavioral health infrastructure across the board.

That third level is where institutions, including employers, schools, governments, and healthcare systems, carry the greatest responsibility and hold the greatest opportunity to create lasting change.

The real test of Mental Health Awareness Week is not how many people wear green on May 14. It is whether organizations and communities are willing to build systems that genuinely help people long after the campaign ends.

We encourage you to read our full editorial, share it widely, and ask the harder question this week: what concrete action are we actually taking?

  <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        " dir="ltr" data-test-id="main-feed-activity-card__commentary">What if one of the most overlooked public health interventions is also one of the simplest? Go outside.

Clinicians, educators, and helping professionals have been observing for years that people are increasingly disconnected from outdoor spaces, movement, sunlight, and unstructured time in nature. At the same time, rates of anxiety, depression, burnout, loneliness, and chronic stress continue to rise.

Of course, being outdoors is not a cure-all. Nature cannot replace therapy, medication, housing stability, healthcare access, or systemic support. But conversations about mental health sometimes become so focused on clinical intervention that we overlook foundational aspects of wellbeing.

Research continues to show that even brief periods of time outside can support mood, stress reduction, attention, cognitive functioning, and emotional regulation. Some studies suggest benefits can begin with as little as 10 minutes outdoors.

What strikes me most is how often people describe the experience in similar ways: feeling calmer, more grounded, more present, and more connected to something beyond themselves.

In a culture increasingly shaped by screens, constant stimulation, productivity pressures, and indoor living, stepping outside can feel almost deceptively simple. Yet simplicity does not make something insignificant. Sometimes support looks like therapy. Sometimes it looks like medication. Sometimes it looks like a community.

And sometimes it looks like sitting on a porch, walking around the block, gardening, watching birds, or feeling sunlight on your face after a difficult day.
Mental health is not built by a single intervention. It is often shaped by many small, consistent experiences that help us reconnect with ourselves, other people, and the world around us.

  <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        " dir="ltr" data-test-id="main-feed-activity-card__commentary">Based on my personal understanding (please correct me if I’m wrong). These are just some of the issues faced in the mental health scene in Singapore.

1. Increasing number of psychologists doesn’t totally solve the growing mental health demands in Singapore

2. Balancing quantity vs quality of counsellors & psychologists trained from academic institutions 

3. In future, there also needs to be regulation of counsellors to ensure the safety and quality of therapy being offered to clients

4. The high costs for academic training to become a qualified mental health professional VS the starting salary of mental health professionals make retention even harder

5. Few or rarely any insurance scheme covers mental health costs such as therapy. People coming from low income backgrounds will prioritise putting food on the table rather than going for therapy (therapy is seen as a luxury).

6. Long waiting time for subsidised mental health treatment, unless you can afford to go to the private route

7. Insufficient placement sites for trainee counsellors/psychologists as some clients do not want to see mental health professionals in training. Then how do they even gain experience? 

<a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fmentalhealth&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">#mentalhealth</a> <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fsingapore&trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">#singapore</a></p>

  <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        " dir="ltr" data-test-id="feed-reshare-content__commentary">The recent and timely article by <a class="link" href="https://sg.linkedin.com/company/todayonline?trk=public_post_reshare-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post_reshare-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">CNA TODAY</a> by Ms <a class="link" href="https://sg.linkedin.com/in/nicole-lam-yi-siu?trk=public_post_reshare-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post_reshare-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">Nicole Lam</a> highlights a growing tension in Singapore’s mental health landscape. Demand is rising, but expansion is not simply about increasing numbers.

From the ground, this is a system design issue.

First, there is a gap in how care is distributed. Not all mental health concerns require a psychologist. Many cases such as stress, adjustment issues, and relational difficulties can be effectively supported by counsellors. 

Strengthening this layer is not about replacement, but about right-siting care appropriately.

If counsellors are to take on a larger role, then registration and licensing frameworks become critical. This protects clients, strengthens professional identity, and builds public trust.

The conversation on affordability also needs nuance. Regulating private practice fees may sound appealing, but it overlooks a key reality. Private counselling is not subsidised, unlike public healthcare.

To many clients, $200 per hour feels expensive. 
To therapists, it is often barely enough to cover the true cost of practice.

Behind each session are constraints that are often not visible:
1.) practical capacity limits. Four clients a day is often the maximum, five is a stretch
2.) cancellations and no-shows that disrupt schedules and income stability
high costs of clinical supervision, rental, and ongoing professional development
3.) Space itself is another constraint. Suitable counselling environments require privacy, accessibility, and consistency. Rental costs and the scarcity of appropriate spaces in Singapore make this difficult to sustain.

This leads to a broader truth. Many practitioners are not operating on high margins. A significant number take on multiple roles such as lecturing, training, or corporate work to maintain a viable income.

In reality, more stable revenue often comes not from therapy, but from workshops, talks, and lectures.

This raises an uncomfortable but important question. If therapy itself is not economically sustainable, what does that mean for long-term access to care?

If the goal is to improve accessibility, then the focus cannot be limited to fee regulation alone. It must also consider:
 - how care is distributed across professional roles
 - how practitioners are supported and regulated
 - how sustainable practice can be maintained without compromising quality

Mental health care is not a volume-driven service. It is relational, intensive, and bounded by human capacity.

The question is not just how we expand, but how we build a system that is both accessible and sustainable for clients and practitioners alike.

Article link - <a class="link" href="https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https%3A%2F%2Flnkd%2Ein%2FgDw6mRZ3&urlhash=xBUH&trk=public_post_reshare-text" target="_self" rel="nofollow" data-tracking-control-name="public_post_reshare-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">https://lnkd.in/gDw6mRZ3</a></p>

  <div class="attributed-text-segment-list__container relative mt-1 mb-1.5 babybear:mt-0 babybear:mb-0.5">
    <p class="attributed-text-segment-list__content text-color-text !text-sm whitespace-pre-wrap break-words
        " dir="ltr" data-test-id="main-feed-activity-card__commentary">I ran a training session yesterday on Mental Health and the Male Brain for the <a class="link" href="https://uk.linkedin.com/company/recoverywellbeingacademy?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">Recovery and Wellbeing Academy</a> and one concept that generated some discussion was the notion of the midlife crisis.

With my academic hat on the science isn’t totally settled. So I thought I would remind myself and share the  arguments:

Arguments FOR the midlife crisis being a “thing:”

The concept was first defined by psychologist Elliott Jaques in 1965 after working with men who experienced a profound change to their identity, drive and mortality during their 40s. Research does exist that supports life satisfaction being “U-shaped” - dipping during middle age and recovering as we get older. There’s something to be said for the unique clustering of challenges around midlife such as caring for children and aging parents, career expectations, identity shifting. We also need to mention hormones and all men are perhaps socialised to ignore their emotions and their fear of not fulfilling their own expectations. It definitely can be, for some men, a legitimate psychological shift.

Arguments AGAINST - or at least for nuance:

A recent study at <a class="link" href="https://de.linkedin.com/school/ulm-university/?trk=public_post-text" target="_self" data-tracking-control-name="public_post-text" data-tracking-will-navigate="">Ulm University</a> analysed data from over 3300 adults and found that life satisfaction actually hits a midpoint in early adulthood and tends to increase with age. Further research has been published that also concluded that only 10–20% of people experience a midlife crisis and when they do “those crises are generally provoked by stressful life events such as job loss or the death of a parent.” Lastly, a 2025 article published in Scientific Reports concluded that “midlife crisis is neither a universal pathology nor a cultural myth” and is instead based on circumstance and environment. Some studies argue that there’s less of an age correlation and more to do with extroversion and people-pleasing.

Either way, what does that mean for men’s mental health?

Whether you want to label it a “midlife crisis” or not is up to you, but the concept behind it is valid. A reckoning with our identity, purpose, loss and mortality. Men have often been conditioned to just “deal” with their emotions. 

The idea behind yesterday’s workshop is about equipping men to build strenght through reaching out for support and using tools that build wellbeing.

Also looking at other genders: 
- Women have their own version but quite frequently compounded by caring responsibilities.
- Gender Diverse and Transgender remains understudied.
